structure_label: Subject pronoun omission in informal speech
core_meaning: Omitting the subject pronoun in informal conversation when context makes it clear.
primary_communicative_fn: Convey action or statement without explicit subject when context is unambiguous.
when_to_use: Use in informal speech and casual writing when the subject is obvious from context or previous statements.
key_variations: Present tense: (Je) parle, (Il) arrive | Passé composé: (Je) suis venu | Imperative: (Tu) regarde!
common_learner_mistakes: Omitting subject in formal writing (incorrect); overusing ellipsis in academic contexts.
chunk_compatibility: Casual dialogue: *(Je) suis fatigué* instead of formal *Je suis fatigué*.
primary_function: Maintain natural rhythm in informal French speech by removing redundant pronouns.
key_forms: Verb conjugation without pronoun: suis fatigué, fait froid, viens, parle français
essential_vocabulary: pronom sujet, omission, contexte clair, informel
why_it_matters: Native speakers drop subject pronouns frequently in casual speech; learning to recognize this improves listening comprehension.
common_mistakes: Learners who avoid subject pronoun ellipsis sound overly formal; those who overuse it in writing sound sloppy.

structure_label: Definite article ellipsis with body parts in possessive context
core_meaning: Using possessive adjectives instead of definite articles when referring to body parts in context of possession.
primary_communicative_fn: Clarify body-part possession using possessive adjectives instead of definite articles.
when_to_use: Use when a body part's possession is contextually clear; the possessive adjective makes the owner explicit.
pattern: Je lève la main (not 'je lève ma main') | J'ai mal à la tête (not 'j'ai mal à la mienne tête')
key_variations: Body parts: main, tête, bras, jambe, pied, oeil | With possessive: ma main, son bras, leur tête
essential_vocabulary_slots: main, tête, bras, jambe, pied, oeil, oreille, nez
common_learner_mistakes: Using both article and possessive (ma la tête); confusion between possessive and article usage.
chunk_compatibility: Body-part expressions: *Lève la main!*, *J'ai mal à la tête*, *Ferme les yeux*.
primary_function: Express body-part relationships using French-specific grammar (definite article over possessive in certain contexts).
key_forms: le/la + body part: le bras, la main, la tête | possessive + body part: ma main, son bras
essential_vocabulary: article défini, possessif, partie du corps, contexte
why_it_matters: French prefers definite articles for body parts when possession is contextually clear; English uses possessives.
common_mistakes: Learners add both article and possessive (error); native speakers use article alone when context is clear.

structure_label: Preposition omission before infinitive after certain verbs
core_meaning: Omitting or including prepositions before infinitives depending on the verb and meaning.
primary_communicative_fn: Control infinitive phrase formation by understanding which verbs require or omit prepositions.
when_to_use: Different verbs require different prepositions or none; some verbs need à, others de, others require no preposition.
pattern: Verb + infinitive: Aller (no preposition) | Commencer + à + infinitive | Cesser + de + infinitive
key_variations: No preposition: aller, pouvoir, devoir, vouloir, faire, laisser | à: commencer, continuer, apprendre | de: cesser, arrêter, finir
essential_vocabulary_slots: aller, pouvoir, devoir, vouloir, faire, laisser, commencer, continuer, cesser
common_learner_mistakes: Adding preposition when not needed (Je veux à aller); omitting required preposition (Il commence travailler instead of commence à).
chunk_compatibility: Fixed expressions: *Je veux aller*, *Commence à travailler*, *Cesse de parler*.
primary_function: Properly form infinitive phrases by applying verb-specific preposition rules.
key_forms: Aller + infinitive, Commencer à + infinitive, Cesser de + infinitive, Pouvoir + infinitive
why_it_matters: Incorrect preposition use changes meaning or sounds unnatural; native fluency requires memorizing verb-preposition pairs.
common_mistakes: Using 'à' with vouloir (Je veux à aller is wrong); forgetting 'de' with finir or arrêter.

structure_label: Relative pronoun ellipsis in casual speech
core_meaning: Omitting relative pronouns 'qui' or 'que' in casual French when meaning is clear from context.
primary_communicative_fn: Express relative relationships without explicit pronoun when context permits.
when_to_use: Use in informal speech only; omit only the direct-object relative pronoun 'que', never 'qui' (subject).
pattern: La femme (que) j'ai vue | Le livre (que) je lis | L'ami (que) tu connais
key_variations: Direct object only: (que) peut être omis | Subject: (qui) CANNOT be omitted | Time/place: (où) CANNOT be omitted
essential_vocabulary_slots: voir, lire, connaître, aimer, trouver, chercher
common_learner_mistakes: Omitting relative pronouns in formal writing (incorrect); omitting subject pronoun 'qui' (wrong grammar).
chunk_compatibility: Casual speech only: *La femme j'ai vue* (informal) vs *La femme que j'ai vue* (correct formal).
primary_function: Maintain casual rhythm in informal French by dropping the direct-object relative pronoun.
key_forms: que-ellipsis: L'homme j'ai rencontré (informal) | qui-retained: L'homme qui parle (always required)
essential_vocabulary: pronom relatif, omission, contexte informel, objet direct
why_it_matters: Recognizing que-ellipsis in native speech improves listening comprehension; using it in writing is an error.
common_mistakes: Dropping 'qui' (subject pronoun—wrong); using this pattern in formal writing (too casual).

core_meaning: Omitting the noun after demonstrative pronouns when the noun is understood from context.
primary_communicative_fn: Refer to items using demonstratives without repeating the noun.
when_to_use: Use when a noun has been mentioned and is understood; the demonstrative pronoun stands alone.
pattern: Ce livre-ci | Celui-ci (that one, understood as a person/object) | Cette robe → celle
key_variations: Simple form: ce, cet, cette, ces | Compound form: celui-ci, celle-là, ceux, celles | Without reference: ce, c'
essential_vocabulary_slots: ce, cet, cette, celui, celle, ceux, celles
common_learner_mistakes: Lacking clear antecedent (confusing reference); confusing demonstratives with possessive pronouns.
chunk_compatibility: Shopping or comparison: *Je préfère celui-ci* (I prefer this one), *Cette robe-ci ou celle-là?*
primary_function: Reference previously mentioned items without repetition using demonstrative pronouns.
key_forms: celui/celle/ceux/celles + -ci/-là: celui-ci, celle-là, ceux-ci, celles-là | ce/c'est used with things
why_it_matters: Native speakers use demonstrative ellipsis constantly in conversation for conciseness.
common_mistakes: Using demonstrative without clear reference (ambiguity); confusing with possessive pronouns (mon vs celui).

structure_label: Verb ellipsis in comparatives and compound sentences
core_meaning: Omitting a repeated verb in the second part of a comparison or coordinated structure.
primary_communicative_fn: Express comparisons or parallel structures without repeating the same verb.
when_to_use: Use in comparatives and parallel constructions where the same verb appears; omit the second occurrence.
pattern: Il travaille plus que moi (omitted: ne travaille) | Elle chante mieux que lui (omitted: ne chante)
key_variations: Comparatives: plus que, moins que, aussi que, mieux que, pire que | Parallel: et..., mais..., ou...
essential_vocabulary_slots: travailler, chanter, courir, parler, faire, avoir, être
common_learner_mistakes: Repeating the full verb unnecessarily; forgetting subject after 'que' in comparative.
chunk_compatibility: Comparatives: *Je travaille plus que toi*, *Elle court mieux que lui*.
primary_function: Avoid redundancy by omitting repeated verbs in parallel or comparative structures.
key_forms: Subject + verb + comparative + que + subject (verb omitted): Il travaille plus que moi
essential_vocabulary: comparatif, ellipse verbale, parallèle, structure coordinée
why_it_matters: Proper verb ellipsis in comparatives sounds natural; repetition sounds awkward to native speakers.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the subject pronoun after 'que' (Je travaille plus que) or repeating the full verb.

structure_label: C'est vs il est with adjectives and nouns
core_meaning: Choosing between 'c'est' (it is) and 'il est' (he/she is) based on grammatical context and meaning.
primary_communicative_fn: Distinguish between personal (il/elle) and impersonal (c'est) copula usage.
when_to_use: Use 'c'est' with noun + adjective combinations; use 'il est' with adjectives only or adjective + infinitive.
pattern: C'est utile | C'est un homme | Il est utile | Il est important de comprendre
key_variations: C'est + adj: C'est bien, C'est difficile | Il est + adj: Il est utile, Il est important | C'est + noun: C'est un professeur
essential_vocabulary_slots: utile, important, difficile, possible, probable, nécessaire, homme, femme, professeur
common_learner_mistakes: Using 'il est' with nouns (Il est un professeur is wrong); using 'c'est' with infinitive only.
chunk_compatibility: Common expressions: *C'est bon*, *Il est important*, *C'est un problème*.
primary_function: Apply correct copula form based on whether statement is personal or impersonal.
key_forms: C'est + adj/noun: C'est bon, C'est une maison | Il est + adj: Il est heureux, Il est jeune | Il est + adj + de + infinitive
essential_vocabulary: c'est, il est, adjectif, nom, copule, impersonnel
why_it_matters: Correct usage is essential; incorrect forms sound unnatural and mark non-native speech.
common_mistakes: *Il est une maison* (wrong—use c'est); *C'est utile* (correct) vs *Il est utile* (both valid in some contexts).

core_meaning: Omitting object pronouns in imperative sentences when the object is understood from context.
primary_communicative_fn: Give commands without explicit object pronouns when context makes the object clear.
when_to_use: Use in imperatives and casual commands where the object has been mentioned or is obvious.
pattern: Regarde! (Look! at what? understood) | Donne! (Give! to whom? understood) | Viens!
key_variations: With object understood: Regarde!, Donne!, Prends! | With object stated: Regarde-moi, Donne-moi, Prends-le
essential_vocabulary_slots: regarder, donner, prendre, faire, écouter, manger, dire
common_learner_mistakes: Over-retaining pronouns in casual speech (sounds formal); using ellipsis where reference is unclear.
chunk_compatibility: Casual commands: *Regarde!*, *Écoute!*, *Viens!* instead of formal *Regarde cela*, etc.
primary_function: Shorten imperative forms by dropping understood object pronouns for natural speech.
key_forms: Imperative alone: Donne! Prends! Écoute! | Imperative + object: Donne-moi! Prends-le!
why_it_matters: Native speakers drop objects in imperatives for brevity; learners who always include them sound overly formal.
common_mistakes: Using ellipsis when reference is unclear (ambiguity); overusing in formal or written contexts.

core_meaning: Using 'ne' in certain formal contexts without negative meaning; it functions only to mark formality.
primary_communicative_fn: Mark formal or subjunctive contexts with 'ne' without adding negation.
when_to_use: Use in formal French with subjunctive, certain expressions, and comparatives; 'ne' has NO negative meaning.
pattern: Il faut que j'y aille avant qu'il ne parte | Je crains qu'il ne vienne
key_variations: Expressions: peu s'en faut, quoi qu'il en soit, à moins que ne | Comparatives: plus...que ne
essential_vocabulary_slots: aller, partir, venir, faire, être, pouvoir, avoir
common_learner_mistakes: Thinking 'ne' adds negation (it doesn't); adding 'pas' after 'ne' in these constructions.
chunk_compatibility: Formal expressions: *Avant qu'il ne parte*, *À moins qu'il ne vienne*.
primary_function: Mark subjunctive or formal contexts with 'ne' as a stylistic/formal marker, not negation.
key_forms: ne + subjunctive: ne parte, ne vienne, ne soit | Fixed: peu s'en faut, quoi qu'il en soit, à moins que ne
essential_vocabulary: ne explétif, subjonctif, formel, sans négation
why_it_matters: Understanding ne explétif helps learners recognize formal written French and subjunctive contexts.
common_mistakes: Adding 'pas' after ne explétif (wrong—it's not a negation); omitting it in formal contexts.

core_meaning: Using infinitive forms after prepositions instead of conjugated verbs or full clauses.
primary_communicative_fn: Simplify dependent clauses by using infinitives after prepositions.
when_to_use: Use infinitive after prepositions (avant de, après, pour, sans, etc.) instead of subjunctive/indicative.
pattern: Avant de partir (not 'avant que partir') | Après avoir mangé | Pour avoir réussi | Sans parler
key_variations: Avant de + infinitive: Avant de partir | Après + past infinitive: Après avoir vu | Pour + infinitive: Pour comprendre | Sans + infinitive: Sans douter
essential_vocabulary_slots: partir, manger, aller, voir, comprendre, faire, avoir, venir
common_learner_mistakes: Using indicative after 'avant que' (requires subjunctive, not infinitive); using subjunctive when infinitive suffices.
chunk_compatibility: Time expressions: *Avant de venir*, *Après avoir mangé*, *Pour réussir*.
primary_function: Express dependent relationships with infinitives when subject is the same in both clauses.
key_forms: Preposition + infinitive: avant de partir, après avoir vu, pour parler, sans douter
why_it_matters: Infinitive after preposition is more concise than subjunctive clauses when the subject is shared.
common_mistakes: Using subjunctive after infinitive-taking prepositions; conjugating after 'avant de', 'après', etc.

core_meaning: Omitting partitive articles (du, de la, des) in very informal or poetic French when quantity is implied.
primary_communicative_fn: Express quantities or generic references without explicit partitive articles.
when_to_use: Use in very informal or poetic speech only; omitting partitives in formal writing is grammatically incorrect.
pattern: Je veux du pain → Je veux pain (very informal) | Il a des amis → Il a amis (only in colloquial)
key_variations: Formal: du pain, de la viande, des fruits | Informal/poetic: pain, viande, fruits
essential_vocabulary_slots: pain, viande, fruits, eau, café, lait, fromage, sucre
common_learner_mistakes: Overusing in formal contexts (sounds careless); removing articles when they're grammatically required.
chunk_compatibility: Poetic or very casual: *Apporte pain et fromage* instead of proper *du pain et du fromage*.
primary_function: Convey quantity or indefinite reference without explicit partitive articles in informal contexts.
key_forms: Partitive kept: du, de la, des + noun | Partitive omitted: noun alone (informal/poetic only)
essential_vocabulary: article partitif, omission, quantité, contexte informel
why_it_matters: Understanding partitive articles helps distinguish formal and informal French.
common_mistakes: Using article ellipsis in formal writing (grammatically wrong); not understanding when to keep vs omit.

core_meaning: Omitting the adjective when the noun is understood and the adjective describes a previously mentioned noun.
primary_communicative_fn: Reference quality or type through adjective alone when noun is contextually clear.
when_to_use: Use when an adjective-noun pair has been mentioned and the noun is understood from context.
pattern: Le nouveau téléphone → Le nouveau (short for 'le nouveau téléphone') | Les riches et les pauvres
key_variations: With noun: le nouveau téléphone | Without noun: le nouveau (understood as téléphone) | Adjective as noun: les riches, les pauvres
essential_vocabulary_slots: nouveau, ancien, grand, petit, riche, pauvre, bon, mauvais
common_learner_mistakes: Creating ambiguity by omitting adjective without clear antecedent; confusing with noun/adjective conversion.
chunk_compatibility: Comparison: *Je préfère le nouveau* (le nouveau [téléphone] understood), *Les riches et les pauvres* (adjectives as nouns).
primary_function: Avoid repetition by dropping the noun when the adjective refers to a previously mentioned noun.
key_forms: Adjective standing alone: le nouveau, la grande, les petits (nouns understood) | Adjective as noun: les riches, les jeunes
why_it_matters: Native speakers use adjective ellipsis for conciseness; recognizing it improves listening comprehension.
common_mistakes: Omitting adjectives without clear context (creates ambiguity); confusing with adjectives used as nouns.

core_meaning: Omitting conjunctions in parallel structures when they would otherwise repeat.
primary_communicative_fn: Maintain rhythm in parallel structures by omitting repeated conjunctions.
when_to_use: Use in lists, parallel sentences, and coordinated structures where conjunctions would repeat.
pattern: Je veux du pain (et) du fromage | Elle chante (et) danse | L'un (et) l'autre
key_variations: With conjunction: et...et, ou...ou, ni...ni | Without: listed items separated by commas | Ellipsis: pain, fromage, beurre
essential_vocabulary_slots: et, ou, ni, mais, car, donc, parce que
common_learner_mistakes: Omitting conjunctions when clarity requires them; over-omitting in formal writing.
chunk_compatibility: Lists: *pain, fromage et beurre* (omitted 'et' before 'fromage'), *Elle court, saute, danse*.
primary_function: Streamline parallel structures by dropping conjunctions for conciseness.
key_forms: With conjunctions: et...et, ou...ou | Without (ellipsis): item, item, item, et item
why_it_matters: Natural French speech omits conjunctions in lists for fluency; learners who repeat them sound unnatural.
common_mistakes: Omitting necessary conjunctions (ambiguity); overusing ellipsis in formal or written contexts.

structure_label: C'est vs il est with adjective ellipsis
core_meaning: Choosing between 'c'est' and 'il est' when the noun or infinitive is implied or ellipsed.
primary_communicative_fn: Correctly apply copula forms when nouns or infinitives are understood from context.
when_to_use: Use 'c'est' for nouns or when something is implied; use 'il est' for adjectives or with infinitive.
pattern: C'est bien (understood: ce que tu dis) | Il est tard (impersonal) | C'est à moi (understood: de décider)
key_variations: C'est + adj (understood noun): C'est bien | Il est + adj (personal): Il est heureux | Il est + adj + de + inf: Il est important de venir
essential_vocabulary_slots: bien, bon, haut, bas, tard, important, nécessaire, possible
common_learner_mistakes: Confusing when to use 'c'est' vs 'il est'; creating ambiguity through ellipsis.
chunk_compatibility: Common responses: *C'est bien!* (That's good!), *Il est tard* (It's late), *C'est à toi* (It's your turn).
primary_function: Apply correct copula when noun, adjective, or infinitive is understood from context.
key_forms: C'est + adj (noun implied): C'est merveilleux | Il est + adj: Il est grand | Il est + adj + de + inf: Il est temps de partir
essential_vocabulary: c'est, il est, ellipse, contexte, copule
why_it_matters: Correct usage is critical; native speakers recognize errors immediately.
common_mistakes: Mixing 'c'est' and 'il est' contexts; creating ambiguity through ellipsis without clear context.

core_meaning: Omitting sentence adverbs (bien sûr, naturellement, certainement) when the statement's meaning is clear.
primary_communicative_fn: Convey certainty or attitude without explicit sentence adverbs when context permits.
when_to_use: Use when sentence adverbs are redundant or context makes the speaker's attitude obvious.
pattern: Bien sûr (que je viens) | Naturellement, il est là | (Certainement), il va gagner
key_variations: With adverbs: Bien sûr que je viens | Without: Je viens | Other forms: Évidemment, Franchement, Malheureusement
essential_vocabulary_slots: bien sûr, naturellement, certainement, évidemment, franchement, malheureusement
common_learner_mistakes: Overusing adverbs when context suffices; removing adverbs that provide crucial nuance or tone.
chunk_compatibility: Casual responses: *Bien sûr!* (Of course!) vs *Oui* alone (less emphatic).
primary_function: Control emphasis and tone by including or omitting sentence adverbs as appropriate.
key_forms: With adverb: Bien sûr que oui, Naturellement, Évidemment | Without: Oui, Oui, je le sais
essential_vocabulary: adverbe modal, certitude, ton, ellipse, contexte
why_it_matters: Sentence adverbs modify the entire statement's tone; including/omitting them changes the message's force.
common_mistakes: Omitting adverbs that convey essential attitude (weakens meaning); using them redundantly (sounds emphatic when not intended).
